content: Metronidazole diffuses into the organism, inhibits protein synthesis by interacting with DNA, and causes a loss of helical DNA structure and strand breakage. Therefore, it causes cell death in susceptible organisms.

title: Biochemistry, DNA Replication -- Clinical Significance -- Antibiotics
content: Many antibiotics antivirals interfere with DNA replication in prokaryotes to prevent the replication of bacterias and viruses and the progression of disease in patients. One example includes fluoroquinolones, which bind to DNA topoisomerases and inhibit their function and preventing replication. Trimethoprim and sulfonamides prevent the formation of DNA precursors such as purines and pyrimidine, preventing DNA formation. Acyclovir acts as a guanosine analog which is monophosphorylated by HSV thymidine kinase; this phosphorylation creates a triphosphate which inhibits viral DNA polymerase by chain termination.

title: Metronidazole -- Mechanism of Action
content: The mechanism of action of metronidazole occurs through a four-step process. Step one is the entry into the organism by diffusion across the cell membranes of anaerobic and aerobic pathogens. However, antimicrobial effects are limited to anaerobes. [6] Step two involves reductive activation by intracellular transport proteins by altering the chemical structure of pyruvate-ferredoxin oxidoreductase. The reduction of metronidazole creates a concentration gradient in the cell that drives uptake of more drugs and promotes free radical formation that is cytotoxic. [7] Step three, interactions with intracellular targets, is achieved by cytotoxic particles interacting with host cell DNA resulting in DNA strand breakage and fatal destabilization of the DNA helix. [8] Step four is the breakdown of cytotoxic products. Metronidazole is also cytotoxic to facultatively anaerobic bacteria like Helicobacter pylori and Gardnerella vaginalis , but the mechanism of action to these pathogens is not well understood. [6]

title: Macrolides -- Mechanism of Action
content: Due to the overprescription of antibiotics, there has been tremendous growth in resistance to many mainstay therapies. Macrolides are no exception to this situation, and many organisms are excessively resistant to them. The primary cause of macrolide bacterial resistance is post-transcriptional methylation of the bacterial 23S ribosomal RNA. This acquired resistance can occur via two mechanisms: it can be plasmid-mediated or chromosomal. [9] Studies show a solid link to genetic mutations in bacteria and the ability to spread these genes via transposable elements. The gene in question enables bacteria to be resistant to the macrolides, lincosamides, and streptogramin groups of antibiotics simultaneously. [10]

content: Acquired Resistance: Bacteria can obtain the ability to resist the activity of a particular antimicrobial agent to which it was previously susceptible. Bacteria can acquire resistance through a new genetic mutation that helps the bacterium survive or by getting DNA from a bacterium that already is resistant. An example is Mycobacterium tuberculosis resistance to rifamycin.

title: Antiparasitic Drugs -- Mechanism of Action -- Antiprotozoal Agents
content: Metronidazole and tinidazole are 5-nitroimidazole derivatives. Metronidazole has both antibacterial and antiprotozoal properties. It is active against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases such as trichomoniasis and giardiasis. Within the parasites, it engages in redox reactions, releasing toxic reactive intermediates, making it effective as a luminal and extra-luminal amoebicide. The metronidazole metabolites are taken up by the deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), leading to the DNA disruption and, consequently, protein synthesis inhibition. Tinidazole has a similar structure to metronidazole and shares a similar activity profile. However, tinidazole has a higher cure rate for protozoal infections and a better toxicity profile than metronidazole.

title: Levofloxacin -- Mechanism of Action
content: Levofloxacin is a bactericidal antibiotic of the fluoroquinolone drug class that directly inhibits bacterial DNA synthesis. Levofloxacin promotes the breakage of DNA strands by inhibiting DNA-gyrase in susceptible organisms, which inhibits the relaxation of supercoiled DNA. Of the fluoroquinolone class, levofloxacin has the most enhanced activity against gram-positive penicillin-sensitive and resistant organisms, notably Streptococcus pneumoniae  and reduced action against gram-negative bacilli, notably Pseudomonas aeruginosa, compared to ciprofloxacin. Levofloxacin has effectiveness against other common respiratory organisms, notably Haemophilus influenzae , Moraxella catarrhalis ,  Legionella spp , Mycoplasma spp ,  and Chlamydia pneumoniae . [5] Levofloxacin also  has a higher in-vitro activity against Mycobacterium tuberculosis and is preferred over the other fluoroquinolones as second-line antitubercular therapy. [7] A growing concern about drug resistance to fluoroquinolones exists worldwide, which can occur through chromosome-encoded or plasmid-mediated mechanisms.

title: Tetracycline -- Mechanism of Action
content: There is a growing concern over bacterial strains that are resistant to tetracycline antibiotics. Bacterial genes that are resistant to tetracyclines are often encoded on plasmids or transferable elements like transposons. There are two well-documented mechanisms of resistance, which include alteration in ribosomal protection proteins or efflux pumps. The former mechanism allows the ribosomes to proceed with protein synthesis regardless of the high intracellular levels of the drug. The latter mechanism consists of various subtypes of transmembrane pumps that drive out solutes, in this instance, antimicrobials, out of the cell to prevent cell death. There is documentation of a third, less studied mechanism of resistance, which is that of tetracycline modification. All of these mechanisms reduce the efficacy of tetracyclines, calling for increased diligence when clinicians prescribe these drugs. [5] [6] [7] [8]

title: Moxifloxacin -- Mechanism of Action
content: Moxifloxacin exerts its pharmacological effect by inhibiting bacterial DNA synthesis and replication. Moxifloxacin specifically targets 2 crucial enzymes involved in the bacterial DNA replication process—DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV. By binding to these enzymes, moxifloxacin prevents their ability to unwind and repair DNA strands, resulting in DNA damage and the inhibition of bacterial growth. This mechanism disrupts the bacteria's capacity to reproduce and survive, rendering moxifloxacin effective against a wide range of bacterial infections. [6]

title: Streptomycin -- Mechanism of Action
content: As with all aminoglycosides, streptomycin is bactericidal and provides interference with ribosomal peptide/protein synthesis. It binds to a side of 16S rRNA located on the smaller 30S component of the bacterial ribosome, inhibiting its functionality and halting further protein synthesis through inhibition of peptide bond formation. Aminoglycosides are hydrophilic, meaning they are not able to penetrate the hydrophobic bacterial cell membrane. An electron transport system used during the cell’s respiratory cycle is needed to accomplish this, hence why aminoglycosides are only active against aerobic bacteria. [3] [6] [7]

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
content: HIV infection is primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and its prevention and treatment rely on a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents that target the viral reverse transcriptase enzyme. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – including tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine (also referenced as emtricitabin), lamivudine (lamivudin), stavudine (stavudin), zidovudine (zidovudin), and dideoxycytidine – act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into viral DNA and cause premature chain termination, forming a core component of combination HIV therapy. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – doravirine, delavirdine (and its mesylate formulation), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine – bind to a distinct site on reverse transcriptase to inhibit viral replication. Doravirine has a specific contraindication when co‑administered with the rifamycin antibiotic rifapentine, reflecting a clinically important drug‑class interaction. Understanding the class membership, mechanisms of action, preventive potential, and contraindications of these NRTI and NNRTI agents is essential for optimizing patient care, selecting effective regimens, managing drug interactions, and reducing the spread of HIV.

Representative NRTIs are abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, tenofovir disoproxil, and dideoxyadenine (to which abacavir is chemically related). Abacavir‑containing combinations (abacavir / lamivudine and abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine) are mapped to dideoxyadenine, reflecting this structural relationship. title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 432
content: Trimethoprim fulfills an antibacterial therapeutic role, serving as an effective agent against susceptible bacterial infections. Chemically, trimethoprim is derived from a pyrimidine parent structure, classifying it within the pyrimidine family of compounds. Its mechanism of action involves antagonism of folic acid metabolism, as trimethoprim acts as a folic acid antagonist that inhibits bacterial dihydrofolate reductase and thereby disrupts folate synthesis essential for bacterial growth. However, the clinical utility of trimethoprim can be compromised by the emergence of bacterial resistance to trimethoprim, which reduces its efficacy in treating infections. Understanding trimethoprim’s antibacterial function, pyrimidine origin, folic acid antagonism, and the potential for resistance is important for optimizing its use in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2751
content: Metronidazole is linked to ADH antagonists, indicating that it shares a broader pharmacological relationship with agents that oppose alcohol dehydrogenase activity. In addition, metronidazole functions as an inhibitor of protein synthesis, disrupting the production of essential proteins in susceptible microorganisms. This dual association underscores metronidazole’s relevance both in modulating pathways related to alcohol dehydrogenase and in exerting antimicrobial effects through inhibition of protein synthesis, which is important for understanding its therapeutic applications and potential drug interactions.

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 661
content: Ciprofloxacin and cefaklor are both characterized by an antibacterial disposition and each serves an antibacterial therapeutic role. In addition to these agents, fosfomycin tromethamine and hygromycin B are identified as having antibacterial dispositions, underscoring their potential utility in antimicrobial therapy. The use of ciprofloxacin is limited by a contraindicated drug class that includes tizanidine, indicating that co‑administration of tizanidine should be avoided. Moreover, the effectiveness of ciprofloxacin may be compromised in the presence of unicellular infections (عدوى وحيدات الخلية), suggesting that such infections can interfere with its antimicrobial activity. Together, these relationships highlight the antimicrobial properties of the listed agents, the importance of recognizing drug‑drug contraindications, and the need to consider concurrent infections that may affect treatment outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1455
content: Integrase inhibitors classified as anti‑infectives for systematic use comprise the antiretroviral agents dolutegravir and raltegravir, both of which are members of this drug class. These medications function by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA integration into the host genome and contributing to effective systemic treatment of HIV infection. Recognizing dolutegravir and raltegravir as members of the integrase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their shared mechanism of action within antiretroviral therapy.
